perturbation.original_text: Maimon agrees during the term of this Agreement not to accept work or enter into a contract or accept an obligation inconsistent or incompatible with Maimon's obligations under this Agreement or with the scope of services to be rendered for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Maimon agrees during the term of this Agreement to try to avoid accepting work or entering into contracts that are possibly inconsistent with Maimon's obligations under this Agreement or with the scope of services to be rendered for the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'try to avoid' and 'possibly inconsistent' weakens the obligation. Maimon is only required to try to avoid potential conflicts, rather than definitively avoid them, creating ambiguity and rendering the obligation less enforceable. The term 'possibly' introduces subjectivity and uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding fiduciary duty and conflict of interest
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CLS Business Corporation Law § 717 requires corporate officers to act in good faith and with the care of an ordinarily prudent person in a like position under similar circumstances. It demands a clear duty of loyalty and avoidance of conflicts of interest. By stating that Maimon only needs to 'try to avoid' conflicts, the amended contract language contradicts this statutory duty, as it suggests that Maimon may not always be required to fully avoid conflicts, failing to meet the required standard of care and loyalty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Business Corporation Law § 717 (2022) - Duty of Directors. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws BSC - Business Corporation Article 7 - Directors and Officers 717 - Duty of Directors. Universal Citation: NY Bus Corp L § 717 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 717. Duty of directors. (a) A director shall perform his duties as a director, including his duties as a member of any committee of the board upon which he may serve, in good faith and with that degree of care which an ordinarily prudent person in a like position would use under similar circumstances. In performing his duties, a director shall be entitled to rely on information, opinions, reports or statements including financial statements and other financial data, in each case prepared or presented by: (1) one or more officers or employees of the corporation or of any other corporation of which at least fifty percentum of the outstanding shares of stock entitling the holders thereof to vote for the election of directors is owned directly or indirectly by the corporation, whom the director believes to be reliable and competent in the matters presented, (2) counsel, public accountants or other persons as to matters which the director believes to be within such person's professional or expert competence, or (3) a committee of the board upon which he does not serve, duly designated in accordance with a provision of the certificate of incorporation or the by-laws, as to matters within its designated authority, which committee the director believes to merit confidence, so long as in so relying he shall be acting in good faith and with such degree of care, but he shall not be considered to be acting in g
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens Maimon's obligation to avoid conflicts of interest. The original text states that Maimon agrees 'not to accept work or enter into a contract... inconsistent or incompatible' with their obligations. The changed text softens this to 'try to avoid accepting work or entering into contracts that are possibly inconsistent.' This creates ambiguity and makes the obligation less enforceable. NY Bus Corp L § 717 states that a director shall perform his duties 'in good faith and with that degree of care which an ordinarily prudent person in a like position would use under similar circumstances.' The modified text, by allowing Maimon to merely 'try to avoid' conflicts, arguably fails to meet the standard of care required by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Maimon also covenants that at any time after the termination of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, he will not use any Confidential Information or divulge or disclose any Confidential Information to any person, firm or corporation, unless such information is in the public domain through no fault of his or except when required to do so by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Maimon also covenants that at any time after the termination of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, he will endeavor not to use Confidential Information or divulge or disclose Confidential Information to any person, firm or corporation, unless such information has become reasonably accessible or except when required to do so by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to' weakens the commitment to confidentiality. The phrase 'reasonably accessible' is subjective and vague, making it difficult to determine when information is no longer considered confidential. This ambiguity weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. Penal Law § 156.30 addresses offenses related to computer tampering and the unlawful duplication of computer material, including trade secrets. The original text prevents Maimon from divulging confidential information. The revised text implies Maimon has an option to decide what 'reasonably accessible' means, weakening the non-disclosure requirement. The penal law states that trade secrets are illegal to divulge. The term 'endeavor' reduces the strict obligation to not reveal trade secrets, which may be a violation of the Penal Law.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the confidentiality obligation. The original text states Maimon 'will not use' Confidential Information, whereas the changed text states he 'will endeavor not to use' Confidential Information and uses the vague phrase 'reasonably accessible'. N.Y. Penal Law § 156.30, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses the unlawful duplication of computer-related material, including trade secrets. The term 'endeavor' reduces the strict obligation, potentially violating the Penal Law by providing an opportunity to divulge what is now considered 'reasonably accessible' information which undermines the original intent of the law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will reimburse Maimon for all reasonable pre- approved out-of-pocket expenses incurred in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may reimburse Maimon for pre-approved out-of-pocket expenses incurred in connection with this Agreement, subject to budgetary considerations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will reimburse' to 'may reimburse' makes reimbursement discretionary rather than mandatory. Adding 'subject to budgetary considerations' further weakens the obligation, introducing uncertainty about whether Maimon will actually be reimbursed, even for reasonable, pre-approved expenses. It does not define what those considerations are or who is in charge of making decisions on budgetary considerations.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Labor Law § 193 addresses deductions from wages. While it doesn't explicitly mandate reimbursement for expenses, the original agreement clearly establishes reimbursement as part of the compensation package. By making reimbursement discretionary, the modified text creates a situation where Maimon's agreed-upon compensation could be effectively reduced at the Company's discretion. The 'budgetary considerations' language introduces an undefined and potentially arbitrary factor that could be used to deny reimbursements, effectively reducing Maimon's compensation, which may violate the spirit of NY Labor Law § 193, especially if those deductions bring Maimon below minimum wage requirements.

Deductions from wages. * 1. No employer shall make any deduction from the wages of an employee, except deductions which: a. are made in accordance with the provisions of any law or any rule or regulation issued by any governmental agency including regulations promulgated under paragraph c and paragraph d of this subdivision; or b. are expressly authorized in writing by the employee and are for the benefit of the employee, provided that such authorization is voluntary and only given following receipt by the employee of written notice of all terms and conditions of the payment and/or its benefits and the details of the manner in which deductions will be made. Whenever there is a substantial change in the terms or conditions of the payment, including but not limited to, any change in the amount of the deduction, or a substantial change in the benefits of the deduction or the details in the manner in which deductions shall be made, the employer shall, as soon as practicable, but in each case before any increased deduction is made on the employee's behalf, notify the employee prior to the implementation of the change. Such authorization shall be kept on file on the employer's premises for the period during which the employee is employed by the employer and for six years after such employment ends. Notwithstanding the foregoing, employee authorization for deductions under this section may also be provided to the employer pursuant to the terms of a collective bargaining agreement. Such authorized deductions shall be limited to payments for: (i) insurance premiums and prepaid legal plans; (ii) pension or health and welfare benefits; (iii) contributions to a bona fide charitable organization; (iv) purchases made at events sponsored by a bona fide charitable organization affiliated with the employer where at least twenty percent of the profits from such event are being contributed to a bona fide charitable organi
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates reimbursement ('will reimburse'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may reimburse, subject to budgetary considerations'). This introduces ambiguity and a potential for denial of reimbursement, effectively allowing deductions from Maimon's compensation. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate NY Labor Law § 193. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'No employer shall make any deduction from the wages of an employee, except deductions which: a. are made in accordance with the provisions of any law or any rule or regulation issued by any governmental agency including regulations promulgated under paragraph c and paragraph d of this subdivision; or b. are expressly authorized in writing by the employee'. The change transforms a guaranteed reimbursement into a discretionary one, possibly violating the law if it results in unauthorized or unlawful deductions.
